segments.s394-g113: به گیتی (prepositional phrase) precedes the verb and sets the location/scene: 'in/unto the world'. بماناد is an archaic optative/third-person-subjunctive form meaning 'may (he/it) remain' and functions as the main verbal wish. یل سرفراز is the noun (یل) followed by its qualifying adjective (سرفراز) — Persian often places the adjective after the noun, so 'a proud/victorious hero' is the desired subject that the verb hopes will remain.
segments.s394-g114: جهان را uses را as the definite/emphatic marker putting 'the world' into focus. به مهر تو is a prepositional phrase ('by/through/for your affection') modifying the wished-for state. بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may it be', here introducing a wish, and نیاز is the nominal predicate ('need' / 'longing'); together بادا نیاز expresses 'may there be need' (i.e. 'may the world need/long for your love').
segments.s394-g115: یکی is an indefinite numeral/adjective 'a certain' or 'one', placed before آتش to mark a single flare/spark. از is the preposition 'from/out of' introducing the source, and تارک گرگسار is a noun phrase where گرگسار follows and qualifies تارک (adjective-after-noun order); together they name the place/part of the head from which the fire issued. (Note: تارک here is a somewhat archaic/poetic term for a part of the head—its exact sense is not covered by the provided sense list.)
segments.s394-g116: برآمد is a past/simple perfective verb 'arose/issued forth' in third person singular. ز (poetic form of از) marks the source 'from', and پیکار اسفندیار is a genitival phrase 'the battle of Esfandiyar' (noun پیکار followed by the possessor/name اسفندیار), so the clause reads 'it arose from Esfandiyar's battle.'
segments.s394-g33: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'. The verb پوشید is 3rd person singular past 'put on/wore'. The phrase places the verb before the subject روز (poetic inversion): literally 'when (he) put on a yellow robe the day' — i.e. 'when the day put on a yellow robe'.
segments.s394-g34: سوی + باختر is a directional phrase 'toward the west'. گشت is the 3rd-person past of گشتن here meaning 'turned/ became' and introduces the resulting state. گیتی فروز is a compound adjective-noun (giti-frouz) meaning 'world-bright' or 'bright world' describing what turned.
segments.s394-g35: سپه برگرفت is 'he raised/took up the army' — سپه (subject) + برگرفت (past, 3rd sing.). The coordinating و links the next clause; بنه بر نهاد is a compact clause 'he placed a benah on (it)'. In poetry بنه can mean a small heap/camp; بر نهاد is 'placed upon' (نهاد = past of نهادن).
segments.s394-g36: ز یزدان نیکی دهش is a noun phrase 'the gift/grant of goodness from God' (ز = 'from/of'). یاد کرد is a common two-word verb phrase where کرد (did/made) takes یاد ('remembrance') — together meaning 'he remembered'. The whole segment reads 'he remembered the goodness-gift from God.'
segments.s394-g37: The fronted phrase شب تیره is an adverbial/time phrase 'in the dark night'. لشکر is the object 'the host/army' and راند is the past verb 'drove/put to flight'; همی is a continuous/aspectual particle 'kept/was still'. شاه at the end is the subject (poetic order): 'the king kept driving the host in the dark night.'
segments.s394-g38: چو introduces 'when'. خورشید is 'the sun' and زرین کلاه is 'a golden helmet/hat'. بفروخت is a past-form verb here used poetically to mark a change of light or appearance (literally 'he sold/parted with' in ordinary usage, but in context it functions as a past action tied to the timing clause — 'when the sun appeared/was manifested' — followed by the implication that the king put on the golden helmet). The line thus links the sun's appearance with donning the golden helmet.
segments.s394-g39: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. یاقوت شد is 'became ruby' (شد = became). روی برج بره is a noun phrase where روی (face/surface) is modified by برج (tower) and the adjective/epithet بره (here 'innocent/naïve' or a poetic epithet); overall 'the face of the tower' that turned ruby-colored.
segments.s394-g40: بخندید is simple past 'laughed'. روی زمین 'on the face of the earth' uses روی as 'surface/face' and زمین 'earth/ground'. یکسره is an adverb 'completely/altogether', so the clause reads 'the whole face of the earth laughed' or 'the earth laughed outright.'
segments.s394-g105: یکی here is an indefinite 'a/certain' placed before the nouns; 'باد و گردی' is a coordinated subject ('a wind and a dust‑cloud'). برآمد is a past tense intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth' that follows its compound subject; سیاه is a predicative adjective placed after the verb (poetic order) describing the rising mass as 'black/dark'.
segments.s394-g106: بپوشید is a past transitive verb meaning 'covered/concealed' (here the dust-cloud is the agent). دیدار is used in the sense 'the sight/view' (literally 'meeting/appearance') and functions as the object of بپوشید; خورشید و ماه are coordinated objects 'the sun and the moon.' Note the verb precedes its object in this poetic line but the dependency is straightforward.
segments.s394-g107: به بالا برآمد is a directional phrase: به + بالا ('towards/up') + past verb برآمد 'rose up'. جهانجوی is an epithet/adjectival compound 'world‑seeking' placed before the head noun مرد ('man/hero'), so the whole phrase means 'the man who seeks the world/ambitious man rose upward.'
segments.s394-g108: چو functions as a simile 'like/as' modifying the manner of the cry; 'رعد خروشان' is a noun + participial adjective 'thunder, roaring' forming the simile 'like roaring thunder.' یکی before نعره is a numeral/adverb meaning 'one/a single' (i.e. 'one cry'); کرد is the past tense auxiliary/main verb 'made/uttered.'
segments.s394-g109: پشوتن is a proper name (subject). بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (here simple past). همی is a small poetic particle; it can mark continuity/emphasis or be an archaic aspectual marker—here it is an emphatic/poetic marker attached to the clause. با سپاه ('with an army') is an instrumental/associative phrase indicating accompaniment.
segments.s394-g110: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said'); کای is a vocative 'O' addressing someone. نامبردار is an epithet 'renowned/famed' placed before شاه 'king.' Note: گفت here is the past tense verb 'said' introducing direct speech.
segments.s394-g111: The clause begins with negative particle نه and expresses incapacity. نه ... پای دارد is an idiomatic construction: 'to have پای (foot/stance)' = 'to have footing / to be able to stand.' با زخم تو 'with/against your wound' is an instrumental phrase specifying the cause/condition. نهنگ (whale) is the subject denied footing and دارد is the present/past auxiliary 'has' used here to state possession/ability in a generalizing negative.
segments.s394-g112: The sentence continues the negation with a series of coordinated nouns introduced by repeated نه 'neither... nor...': ترک (Turk), جادو (magic/sorcery), شیر و پلنگ (lion and leopard). This is a simple coordinate list under the same negative scope established earlier.
segments.s394-g41: سپه را: سپه is the direct object "the army" and را is the definite/object marker, so "the army" (not indefinite). همه follows and emphasizes totality, so together this phrase means "the whole army". بر + proper name + سپرد = "entrusted to Peshotan" (بر here functions as “to/toward”), with سپرد the past tense verb "entrusted/handed over."
segments.s394-g42: یکی introduces an unspecified individual "one (of them)". جام زرین is noun + adjective (classical/poetic order often puts adjective after noun): "a golden cup." پر از is a fixed adjectival phrase "full of," with از marking the content; می‌ببرد is a present-form verb used narratively here to mean "carried off / took away."
segments.s394-g43: یکی = "someone / another." ساخته here qualifies the instrument—"made"/"well-made" tanbur— and نیز = "also / too," so the clause reads "another (also) wanted a made tanbur." خواست is the simple past "wanted / requested."
segments.s394-g44: همی is an emphatic/poetic particle (here "just/then"), رزم = "battle," and پیش آمدش is a light-verb construction meaning "came before/approached him" (پیش = before/near, آمدش = 'came to him'). سور خواست is a simple clause "he asked/wanted a feast." The sequence shows a temporal/causal relation: as the battle approached, he asked for a banquet.
segments.s394-g45: یکی بیشه‌ای دید: a simple past clause "one saw a grove." همچون بهشت is a simile "like paradise" (همچون = "like/as"). Note بیشه‌ای is indefinite (the -ی) so "a grove."
segments.s394-g46: تو گفتی here functions idiomatically as a generic/impersonal formula "one would say" or "you might say," not a literal address; سپهر = "the sky/heavens," اندر او = "in it/therein," and لاله کشت conveys the idea that tulips were sown or grew there. The verbal sense of گفتی and the verbal sense of کشت are not represented in the provided sense lists, so they are flagged accordingly.
segments.s394-g47: ندید is the negative past form "did not see." از درخت اندر او آفتاب is a prepositional grouping: از (= through/from) درخت (= the trees) اندر او (= within it) آفتاب (= the sun / sunlight). Together: "He saw no sun through the trees inside it."
segments.s394-g48: به هر جای = "at/in every place/spot" (به marking location), بر چشمه‌ای = "on/by a spring" or simply "there was a spring" with the indefinite چشمه‌ای, and چون گلاب = "like rosewater" (چون = like). The phrase describes that everywhere one found a spring resembling rosewater.
segments.s394-g49: فرود آمد is a light verbal phrase formed by the noun فرود + آمد (literally 'descent came') meaning 'he alighted/descended'. از بارگی is a simple prepositional phrase 'from/out of the foliage' (بارگی = foliage/leaf). چو سزید is an adverbial clause of manner: چو = 'as' and سزید (past of سزایدن) = 'was fitting/deserved', so together 'as was fitting'.
segments.s394-g50: ز بیشه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the grove' (ز = از). لب چشمه‌ای is the noun phrase 'the bank/edge of a spring' (لب = brink/edge; چشمه‌ای = 'a spring' with the indefinite suffix). برگزید is the simple past transitive 'he chose', so the clause reads 'from the grove he chose the edge of a spring'.
segments.s394-g51: یکی before جام is an indefinite determiner 'a certain' or 'one' (introducing the object). جام زرین is the noun phrase 'a golden cup'. به کف برنهاد is a verb phrase: به + کف = 'into/onto the palm' and برنهاد (past) = 'he placed/put', so 'he placed a golden cup into/on his palm.'
segments.s394-g52: چو دانست is a temporal clause 'when he knew/realized' (چو = 'when', دانست = past 'he knew'). کز introduces the subordinate content/cause clause here ('from/that'), followed by می‌دلش (his wine‑heart/desire for wine) and گشت شاد 'became glad' (گشت = 'became', شاد = 'glad'). Together: 'when he knew that, from wine, his heart had become glad.'
segments.s394-g53: همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately/at that moment'. تنبور را is the definite direct object (تنبور = the tanbur, را = object marker). برگرفت is the past transitive 'he took up' or 'he seized', so the clause means 'he immediately took up the tanbur.'
segments.s394-g54: سراییدن و ناله اندر گرفت uses a compound construction where two verbal nouns (سراییدن = 'to sing/compose', ناله = 'lament') are coordinated by و and followed by اندر گرفت. In classical usage X اندر گرفتن (here گرفت) means 'to take up/begin X', so the phrase means 'he began singing and lamenting' or 'he took up singing and laments.'
segments.s394-g55: همی گفت is a narrative formula 'thus/continually he said' (همی often gives a continuous or emphatic sense; گفت is the past of 'say'). بداختر is an epithet 'ill‑fated/ill‑starred' modifying the name اسفندیار. The whole segment is 'thus spoke ill‑fated Esfandiyar.'
segments.s394-g56: که introduces reported content 'that'. هرگز نبیند is a negative temporal statement 'he never sees/shall see' (نبیند here used in narrative as a negative present/subjunctive meaning 'will not see' or 'never sees'). می‌و میگسار are coordinated nouns 'wine and wine‑companion' (or cupbearer); together the subordinate clause means 'that he will never behold wine or a wine‑companion.'
segments.s394-g1: از آن کار پر درد شد = a causal prepositional phrase + predicate. از here marks cause ('because of that deed'); آن کار ('that deed') is the cause. پرِ درد is an adjectival phrase 'full of pain/grief' placed before the past-tense copula شد ('became'). گرگسار (the subject) comes at the end: literally 'Because of that deed, (the) wolf-haunted place became full of grief.'
segments.s394-g2: کجا زنده شد مرده اسفندیار = a rhetorical locative question. کجا 'where' opens the question; زنده here characterises the person as 'alive' (the former state), شد + مرده = 'became dead' (past change of state), and اسفندیار is the subject placed at the end. Word order is poetically inverted: 'Where did Esfandiyār, who was living, become dead?'
segments.s394-g3: سراپرده زد بر لب آب شاه = noun + past verb + locative phrase. سراپرده 'the inner curtain/inner chamber' appears before the verb; زد is simple past 'struck' (third person). بر لب آب شاه is the location 'on/at the king's water‑brink.' The subject is implied/omitted (typical in narrative), so the clause reads '(someone) struck the inner curtain at the king’s waterside.'
segments.s394-g4: همه خیمه‌ها گردش اندر سپاه = subject + nominal predicate + locative phrase. همه خیمه‌ها 'all the tents' is the topic; گردش functions as a verbal‑noun/predicate meaning 'movement/turning' (i.e., 'were in motion' or 'moved about'); اندر سپاه is the location 'within/among the host.' Poetic word order places the predicate before or beside its locative modifier.
segments.s394-g5: می‌و رود بر خوان و میخواره خواست = a compact poetic coordination. می‌و (wine/fruit) + رود (here part of the banquet items placed 'on the table') + بر خوان 'on the table' = 'wine and fruit on the table.' The conjunction و links the next clause: میخواره 'the wine‑drinker/tippler' appears before the verb خواست, which is past 'he desired/summoned.' In poetry objects or people addressed often precede the verb, so 'he summoned the tippler.'
segments.s394-g6: به یاد جهاندار بر پای خاست = prepositional phrase + idiomatic verb. به یاد 'in remembrance/of memory of' governs جهاندار 'the sovereign' (the one remembered). بر پای خاست is an idiom meaning 'rose to his feet' (بر = 'on', پای = 'foot', خاست = past 'rose'). The clause as a whole: 'remembering the sovereign, he stood up.'
segments.s394-g7: بفرمود تا داغ دل گرگسار = بفرمود is an honorific/simple past 'he ordered/commanded.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'). داغ دل 'the burning‑grief/wound of the heart' is the object of the subordinate clause and گرگسار qualifies that heart ('of the wolf‑haunted place' or 'of the wolf‑ridden one'). The subordinate clause is completed by the verb in the following segment (typical chaining across hemistichs).
segments.s394-g8: بیامد نوان پیش اسفندیار = verb + subject + locative phrase. بیامد 'came' is past tense placed first (poetic inversion); نوان 'a youth / youthful one' is the coming subject; پیش اسفندیار 'into the presence of Esfandiyār' is the locative. Together: 'a youth came before Esfandiyār.' The verb earlier (in previous segment) completes the subordinate clause begun by تا.
segments.s394-g17: تو (you) is the explicit subject; فردا (tomorrow) is a temporal adverb. چو ... آیی forms a subordinate temporal clause ('when you come') with چو = 'when' and آیی (2nd/3rd-person present/subjunctive form of آمدن) = 'you come'. فرود here functions as an imperative/elliptical command 'alight / come down' placed after the clause for emphatic effect.
segments.s394-g18: به پیشت is a directional/prepositional phrase 'toward your presence' (به + پیش + enclitic -ت). زن جادو is a compound subject 'a woman of magic' = 'sorceress'. آرد is 3rd-person singular present (classical form of آوردن) 'brings' and درود is the object 'greeting'. Word order is prepositional phrase → subject → verb → object.
segments.s394-g19: که is the subordinating particle introducing a reported/nominal clause ('that'). دیدست is an impersonal/classical perfect-like form 'it appears/it has been seen'. ز این پیش = 'before this / previously'. لشکر بسی places the noun (لشکر 'host') with the qualifier بسی ('much/many') following it in poetic order: 'many a host'.
segments.s394-g20: نکردست is a negative perfect/compound meaning 'has not done' or 'there is not (one who)'. پیچان روان is a nominal/participial phrase 'twisting movement / turning course' (پیچان modifying روان). از کسی marks the agent/author with از + کسی; in combination with the negation it means 'by no one'. Together: 'no one has caused a turning/deflection.'
segments.s394-g21: The first چو ('when') plus خواهد ('wishes/wills') form a temporal clause 'when it wills'. بیابان ... چو دریا کند is a simile: the second چو functions as 'like/as' and کند (3rd-singular) is 'makes/does' — literally 'it makes the desert like the sea'. The subject is implied (the thing previously spoken-of).
segments.s394-g22: به بالای خورشید is a prepositional phrase indicating extent/height 'up to/above the sun'. پهنا is a noun 'breadth/expanse' and کند is the verb 'it makes / it spreads' — so the clause means 'it spreads (its) expanse to the height of the sun.' Word order places the prepositional phrase before the object and final verb, typical in poetry.
segments.s394-g23: ورا is a dative/accusative marker 'to/at him' (marks the person affected). غول خوانند is a verbal clause 'they call (him) ghoul' — خوانند is 3rd-person plural classical form 'they call'. شاهان is the agent/subject 'the kings' and به نام ('by name' / 'by that name') indicates the manner of calling.
segments.s394-g24: به‌روز جوانی here functions as a vocative: به‌روز is a proper name (Beh-ruz) addressed together with جوانی 'young man / youth'. مرو is the negative imperative 'do not go' and پیش دام is a locative/prepositional phrase 'before the snare / in front of the trap' — together: 'O Beh-ruz, young man, do not go before the snare.'
segments.s394-g25: به introduces a direction/relation ('to/for') with پیروزی اژدها = 'the dragon's victory'. باز + گرد here form the verbal compound 'بازگرد' (باز + گرد) meaning 'return/turn back'; باز is 'again' and گرد is the verbal element 'go/turn'. The phrase functions as the action that the following modal نباید negates.
segments.s394-g26: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not / should not' that negates the preceding action. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows (reason or specification). نام اندر آری به گرد is a subordinate clause in which نام 'name/fame' is the theme; اندر 'in/among' locates it, آری is an archaic form not listed here (it functions like a verb 'bring/make appear' in classical usage), and به گرد is an adverbial 'about/around' ('to put about').
segments.s394-g27: جهانجوی is the speaker's title/epithet ('world-seeker') and گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (classical past). ای introduces a vocative 'O!', and بد شوخ روی is a compound attributive phrase modifying the person addressed: بد 'bad/ill', شوخ 'playful/impudent', روی 'face' used as a kenning for 'person'. Word order (adjectives before noun) is normal in classical Persian vocatives.
segments.s394-g28: ز is the poetic form of 'from/of' and introduces the phrase 'ز من هرچه بینی' = 'whatever of me you see'. بینی is the present/subjunctive 2nd-person form 'you see' used in a relative/indefinite clause. تو is the explicit subject pronoun 'you' (optional emphasis). فردا بگوی places the verb بگوی (imperative/subjunctive 'say/tell') after the time adverb 'tomorrow' — typical classical order: 'whatever you see of me, you (shall) say tomorrow.'
segments.s394-g29: که begins a subordinate clause ('that'). من is the explicit subject 'I'. با introduces an instrumental phrase 'با زن جادوان' = 'with the (magical/enchanted) woman' (here جادوان is adjectival, 'enchanted' or 'witch-like'). آن is a pronominal demonstrative 'that' referring back to something mentioned earlier, and کنم is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative of کردن used to express intent or future action ('I will do / I shall do').
segments.s394-g30: Another clause introduced by که gives purpose/result: 'that I may break ...'. پشت و دل جادوان is the accusative phrase 'the back and the heart of the jādavān' (here jādavān is plural 'enchanters' or collective 'the enchantments/enchanted ones'). بشکنم is the 1st-person subjunctive of شکستن 'to break' expressing the intended outcome ('may I break / I will break').
segments.s394-g31: به پیروزی is a prepositional phrase 'to/for victory' used to mark the domain of the following past-participial modifier. دادده is a past-participle/adjectival form 'given/bestowed' (i.e., 'who has given'), so به پیروزی دادده خدای = 'a god who has granted victory'. The whole phrase modifies or apposes the speaker.
segments.s394-g32: سر جادوان is the direct object 'the head of the enchanters'. اندر + آرم is an older verb phrase: اندر 'into/among/at' plus آرم (here a 1st-person verb 'I put/lay/place' in an archaic form) — the given senses list does not include this verbal meaning, so it's marked outside the provided senses. به پای is the usual prepositional phrase 'at (my) feet'. The clause as a whole means 'I will (or I do) set the enchanters' head at my feet.'
segments.s394-g89: بدان آهن از جان اسفندیار is a prepositional/nominal phrase that locates the action with respect to Esfandiyār: از جانِ اسفندیار = 'from Esfandiyār's life/soul'. بِدان / بدان here functions as a classical/poetic locative/relational particle ('by/with/that') attaching to آهن 'the iron' (poetic for weapon). Word order is topical: the instrument/thing (بدان آهن) comes before the source phrase (از جانِ اسفندیار).
segments.s394-g90: نبردی here is an archaic/past narrative form of بردن meaning 'took/removed' (third-person past in epic diction). گمانی به بد روزگار is the object: گمانی 'a suspicion/foreboding' qualified by the prepositional phrase به بدّ روزگار 'of/for ill fate'. The verb precedes its object in this poetic word order, so the clause reads '[it] took a foreboding concerning ill fortune.'
segments.s394-g91: بینداخت is the past tense (he cast/threw) of انداختن with the prefix بیـ used in classical style; it governs the direct object زنجیر 'chain'. در گردنش is a locative phrase 'around his neck' where در = 'in/around' and -ش is the enclitic pronoun 'his'. The verb + object + locative order is regular for narrative past.
segments.s394-g92: بر آن سان که is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'in such a manner that / so that'; it introduces a result or purpose clause. نیرو ببرد is a present/subjunctive/resultive clause with ببرد (بردن) 'take/carry away' meaning 'it would carry away/take away strength' and از تنِش 'from his body' marks the source; the enclitic -ش again means 'his'.
segments.s394-g93: زن جادو is a compound nominal phrase 'the (female) magician / sorceress' (زن 'woman' + جادو 'magic'). از خویشتن شیر کرد is a causative/result clause: از خویشتن 'from/of herself' + [NOUN] کردن = 'to make (oneself) [a NOUN]'; شیر کرد therefore means 'she made herself a lion' or 'turned into a lion', with کرد as the past auxiliary/lexical verb.
segments.s394-g94: جهانجوی is an epithet 'the world‑seeker' (an ambitious or fame‑seeking warrior) functioning as subject. آهنگِ شمشیر کردن is an idiom: آهنگ 'intention/aim' + شمشیر 'sword' + کردن 'to make/do' — together meaning 'to make a move/attack with the sword' or 'to set his intention on the sword' (i.e. prepare/launch an attack). Word order (epithet + action) is typical of epic narration.
segments.s394-g95: بدو گفت is the ditransitive clause 'he said to him' where بدو = 'to him' and گفت is the past of گفتن 'said' (classical narrative past). بر من نیاری گزند is the quoted content or object clause: بر من = 'against me / to me' (target), نیاری is an archaic negative-future/subjunctive form meaning 'you will not bring' or 'you shall not bring' (classical 2nd‑person form), and گزند = 'harm'. The sequence is an object clause conveying 'you will not harm me.'
segments.s394-g96: اگر introduces a conditional 'if/though'. آهنین کوه گردی بلند is a compact, archaic hypothetical: آهنین 'iron/steely' + کوه 'mountain' + گردی is an older/poetic second‑person copular/verb form ('you are' / 'you become'), and بلند 'high/lofty' qualifies the noun. Together: 'if you are an iron (thing), a high mountain' — a hyperbolic conditional used to stress 'even if you were as mighty as an iron mountain...'.
segments.s394-g97: بیارای is an imperative/command (addressing the addressee) meaning 'array/prepare/adorn yourself'. زآن سان که is a fixed subordinating phrase 'in such a way that/so that'; here که introduces the subordinate clause. هستی is the 2nd-person present of بودن ('you are'), and رخت ('clothing, gear') is the object/predicate: together زآن سان که هستی رخت = 'so that you are clothed'.
segments.s394-g98: به + noun (شمشیر) marks instrument/means here: 'by/with the sword'. یازم is a first-person verbal form meaning 'I open/strike/break forth' (here 'I will (answer) with a sword'); کنون = 'now' sets the time; پاسخت is the possessive form 'your answer/response' (literally 'your answer'), object of the verb.
segments.s394-g99: به زنجیر شد is a passive/resultative expression: به + زنجیر + شد (شد as 'became/was made') = 'was put in chains'. گنده modifies پیری (here used as a substantive for an old man/old age) = 'great/huge (aged one)'; تباه is a predicate adjective 'ruined/destroyed', describing his state.
segments.s394-g100: سر و موی is a coordinated noun phrase 'head and hair'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', so چون برف = 'like snow' describes the hair (i.e. white as snow). و رنگی سیاه links another descriptive predicate: رنگی (a nominal/participial form meaning 'a coloring/complexion') modified by سیاه = 'and a black complexion'. The adjectives follow the nouns they qualify.
segments.s394-g101: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'. تیز خنجر is a noun phrase 'a sharp dagger' (تیز qualifying خنجر). بزد is the simple past/perfective 'he struck/smote'. بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'on his head' (بر + direct object سر + enclitic possessive ش = 'his head').
segments.s394-g102: مبادا که is a cautionary/subjunctive formula 'lest' or 'may it not be that'. بینی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you see' used in the prohibitive formula (i.e. 'do not let it be that you see...'). سرش‌گر should be read as سرش + گر (گر = 'if'); this conditional phrase 'if his head...' is completed by برش, here a nominal/participle 'cut'/'cut off' — together: 'lest you see his head if (it is) cut' ≈ 'lest you see his head cut off.'
segments.s394-g103: چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. جادو (in context) names the sorcerer/enchanter who is the subject; بمرد is a past verb 'died' (here 'he died'). آسمان تیره گشت is an independent clause 'the sky became dark' (تیره 'dark', گشت 'became/turned').
segments.s394-g104: بر آن سان که is a connective phrase 'in that manner/so that' introducing the result clause. چشم is the subject 'the eye/eyes'; اندر آن = 'in/at it' (location/target of the gaze). خیره گشت uses خیره ('staring, transfixed') plus گشت ('became') to mean 'became transfixed/stunned', describing the effect on the eye(s).
segments.s394-g57: نبیند is a third‑person present/optative form of یافتن/دیدن with the negative/irrealis prefix ن-, here functioning as 'will not see' / 'shall see nothing'. جز … از introduces the exception ('except (for) …'); از is the poetical preposition 'from/of'. شیر ('lion') and نر اژدها ('male dragon') form the noun phrase that is the only thing she will see.
segments.s394-g58: ز here means 'from' (poetic). چنگ (claw) takes the genitive plural بلاها ('calamities/afflictions'), so ز چنگ بلاها = 'from the claws of calamities.' نیابد is the same root یاب in a third‑person negative/irrealis form 'will not find' (i.e. 'cannot find'); رها is a nominal/adjectival complement meaning 'freedom / being free' and is the thing not found — 'find no release.'
segments.s394-g59: نیابد is again the negative/irrealis form of یاب 'find/obtain' ('will not obtain' / 'one does not obtain'). همی is an adverbial particle adding habitual/continuing sense ('ever/indeed' in poetic register). ز این جهان بهره‌ای is an indefinite partitive phrase 'any share/portion of this world' (بهره‌ای = 'a share/any share'), the object of نیابد.
segments.s394-g60: به marks the means or occasion ('by / at'); دیدار here means 'beholding / sight' or 'meeting.' فرخ is an adjective 'fortunate/fortunate‑looking' modifying پری چهره‌ای, where پری ('a fairy' / beautiful woman) + چهره ('face') with the indefinite suffix -ای gives 'a fairy‑faced (one)' or 'the fair, fairy‑faced one.' So the whole phrase means 'by the sight of the fortunate, fairy‑faced woman.'
segments.s394-g61: بیابم is first‑person singular subjunctive/optative of یاب 'may I obtain / let me obtain' (an optative wish). ز یزدان = 'from God' (agent/source), and همی here adds a poetic/emphatic quality ('indeed / surely'). کام دل is a genitive phrase 'the wish/desire of the heart' serving as the direct object of بیابم.
segments.s394-g62: مرا‌گر is a compacted poetic ordering of 'مرا گر' = 'if (it is) to me' (گر = 'if', an archaic/poetic conditional particle). دهد is third‑person present/subjunctive of دادن 'gives / grants'. چهره دلگسل is the object 'a face that rends the heart' (دلگسل = 'heart‑rending'). The clause is a conditional ('if He gives me that heart‑rending face'), linked to the wish in the previous segment.
segments.s394-g63: به بالا = 'on high / above' (locative adverbial). چو is the simile particle 'like/as'; here it repeats to give two comparisons: چو سرو ('like a cypress') and چو خورشید ('like the sun'). روی (face) comes at the end as the noun being described — a typical poetic word order where similes and modifiers surround or follow the head noun.
segments.s394-g64: فروهشته is a past‑participle/adjectival verb meaning 'scattered / let fall (down)' describing the hair/perfume. از مشک 'from/with musk' indicates that musk (the perfume) is involved; تا introduces the extent 'as far as / down to' and پای موی here means 'the ends/tips (literally “foot”) of the hair.' Together: 'musk was scattered down to the ends of her hair.'
segments.s394-g73: بیامد is a classical past‑tense verb (3rd singular) meaning 'he came.' The preposition به precedes the location phrase; نزدیک functions as an adjective/noun 'near' and governs the name اسفندیار. Word order here (verb + prepositional phrase) is typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s394-g74: نشست is the past tense 'sat (down)' (verb). The sequence از بر + noun is an archaic/poetic locative: از بر (literally 'from/on') marks the place 'by/at the side of' — سبزه و جویبار ('the green and the stream') is the location. Note the compact, slightly inverted classical order of prepositions and nouns.
segments.s394-g75: جهانجوی is an epithet/name ('world‑seeker') serving as the subject. چون here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/as' introducing the clause. The object phrase روی او را ('his face' + object marker را) precedes the verb بدید ('saw'), a normal classical order: subject + temporal conjunction + object را + verb (past).
segments.s394-g76: سرود here is a past action meaning 'he sang' / 'he composed a song.' The conjunction و links further actions/objects. می‌و and رود form a poetic collocation (here read as 'wine' or its figurative sweetness and a string instrument, the lute). برتر functions adverbially 'higher' / 'more lofty', and کشید is the past verb 'drew/raised/played' (classical کشیدن used for pulling, drawing forth, or playing an instrument). The line lists actions/objects compactly in poetic sequence.
segments.s394-g77: چنین گفت is a fixed narrative phrase 'thus (he) said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' in context). کای is a vocative particle 'O', followed by دادگر ('the just one' / 'just ruler') as the addressee; یک خدای is an emphatic/poetic phrase 'one God' or 'a single godlike one' used appositively or as further address. The cluster is a direct address (vocative) embedded after the reporting phrase.
segments.s394-g78: به introduces the destination/realm ('to/into') — کوه و بیابان ('mountain and wilderness') are the places. توی is the older/prepositional 'in/among'. رهنمای (noun 'guide') here functions as an imperative/vocative idea ('be (my) guide' or 'be guiding me'); classical Persian often omits an explicit copula/imperative auxiliary, so the noun expresses the petition/request for guidance.
segments.s394-g79: بجستم is a first‑person past form (poetic/archaic) meaning roughly 'I sought/found/rose' depending on context (here 'I have found/come upon'); هم‌اکنون is the adverb 'right now.' پری چهره‌ای is an adjectival noun phrase 'a fairy‑faced one' (پری = 'fairy' → 'fairy‑faced', چهره‌ای indefinite suffix indicating 'one with a face like a fairy').
segments.s394-g80: به + تن شهره‌ای is a compact classical phrase: به تن شهره‌ای = 'renowned/famed in body' (شهره‌ای = 'famous', here qualifying 'her body' or 'in bodily fame'). زو ('from her', poetic contraction of از او) introduces the source; مرا (here segmented as م + را) is the first‑person object 'to me' (م = me, را = object marker). بهره‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a share/portion'. Together the clause means 'from her there is to me a portion' — i.e. 'I have my share from her.'
segments.s394-g65: This is a compact noun phrase: 'زنِ جادو' (the woman of magic / sorceress) is the subject and 'آواز اسفندیار' is the thing she hears (object). Classical/poetic Persian often places two noun phrases side by side without the ezafe; here 'آواز اسفندیار' functions as a genitive phrase 'Esfandiyar's voice.'
segments.s394-g66: 'چو' at the start is a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'بشنید' is a simple past 'she/he heard' (3sg). 'شد' is the past of 'شدن' here meaning 'became' (change of state). The second 'چون' is a simile 'like', followed by 'گل' (flower) and the locative/temporal phrase 'اندر بهار' = 'in spring.' Word order is typical older Persian: temporal conj. + verb + copula + simile phrase.
segments.s394-g67: 'چنین گفت' functions as a reporting formula 'thus it was said' or 'thus he said.' 'کآمد' is an archaic/poetic contracted form equivalent to 'که آمد' or 'it came/arrived' (introducing the reported clause). 'هژبری' is a proper name (the subject of 'کآمد'), followed by the prepositional phrase 'به دام' = 'into the trap.'
segments.s394-g68: A coordinated list followed by a participial phrase: 'ابا چامه و رود' are coordinated items (the exact identities are names/words in the poem) joined by 'و'. The phrase 'پر کرده جام' is a compound in which 'پر' = 'full (of)' modifies the perfect participle 'کرده' + object 'جام' yielding 'having filled the cup' or 'filled the cup.' The sequence lists what (or who) filled the cup and then states the result.
segments.s394-g69: 'پر' here is a preposed adjective meaning 'full (of)' and modifies the following noun 'آژنگ' to form 'پرِ آژنگ' = 'full of azhang' which qualifies 'رویی' (face/appearance). 'بی آیین و زشت' is a negative attribute 'without custom/ceremony and ugly' (bi + noun = without X). The whole segment describes the face onto which something is done.
segments.s394-g70: This gives the action done to that face. 'بدان' (classical contraction roughly 'به آن' / 'on that' or 'thereupon') marks the target/locative; 'تیرگیِ جادویها' is a noun phrase 'the darkness/gloom of the sorceries' (tiregi modifying the plural jādūyāhā). 'نوشت' is the past tense 'wrote/inscribed.' Word order places the locative/affected phrase before the verb, as is common in older Persian.
segments.s394-g71: 'به سان' = 'in the manner of / like.' 'یکی ترک' = 'a certain Turk' (یکی = an/one, ترک = Turk, here an epithet). 'شد' is the past of 'شدن' used as an intransitive 'appeared / became.' 'خوب روی' is an adjective+noun 'good-faced' = 'fair/beautiful-faced.' The phrase describes someone appearing who has a Turk's form but a beautiful face.
segments.s394-g72: This is a pair of similes describing appearance: 'چو' = 'as/like', 'دیبای چینی' = 'Chinese brocade/silk' modifies 'رخ' (face) as a simile, and the second part 'از مشک موی' uses 'از' = 'of/from' to link 'مشک' (musk) to 'موی' (hair) meaning 'hair of musk' i.e. hair scented with musk. Classical word order places the similes immediately after the noun they qualify.
segments.s394-g9: می‌خسروانی is a poetic/compound expression meaning ‘in kingly fashion’ or ‘the kingly/drinking one’ (adverbial/epithet). سه جامش is a noun phrase 'his three cups' (جام = cup/draught) with the possessive -ش. بداد here is an older past-tense verb form meaning 'gave' (word order: subject implied + object + verb), though the listed noun-sense doesn't match the verbal use.
segments.s394-g10: بخندید is 3rd-person past 'he laughed.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' زآن ('from that') introduces the source/cause. اژدها کرده یاد is an archaic/periphrastic way to say 'remembered the dragon': یاد (noun 'remembrance') followed by کرد ('made/did') yields 'made remembrance' → 'remembered'; the object (اژدها) precedes the compound predicate.
segments.s394-g11: بدو گفت is an indirect-speech construction 'he said to him' where بدو is a dative/locative 'to him' and گفت is the matrix verb 'said' (the listed senses for گفت do not include the verbal sense here). کای is the vocative 'O.' بد تن بی‌بها is a sequence of adjectives/nouns meaning roughly 'ill/evil-bodied, of no value' (poetic insult); adjective order and compounds are idiomatic and somewhat archaic.
segments.s394-g12: ببین is the imperative 'look' / 'see.' این دم is an idiom 'this moment' / 'now' (دم = moment). آهنج here is a poetic noun 'tone/cry/strain' (used figuratively); نر اژدها is a noun phrase 'the male dragon' ('نر' qualifying اژدها). Word order places demonstrative + temporal noun before the predicate, producing 'Look — at this moment the dragon's male-cry.'
segments.s394-g13: از این پس is an adverbial phrase 'from this onward / henceforth' with پس = 'after/now on.' به منزل is a prepositional phrase 'to the dwelling/stage' (منزل can mean dwelling or a stage/stopping-place). چه پیش آیدم is a rhetorical/subjunctive-like clause 'what will befall me' or 'what will happen to me' where پیش + آیدم expresses 'befall' and آیدم is 1st-person present/future form 'I come' used idiomatically.
segments.s394-g14: کجا is the interrogative 'where.' رنج و تیمار is a coordinated noun phrase 'suffering and care/treatment.' بیش modifies the clause and means 'more' / 'greater.' آیدم again is 'will come to me' / 'will befall me' (1st-person), so the whole segment asks 'Where will more suffering and care befall me?'
segments.s394-g15: بدو گفت again is 'he said to him' with بدو dative 'to him' and گفت 'said' (the lexical senses list doesn't show the verbal sense). کای is the vocative 'O,' followed by the title شاه 'king' and the epithet پیروزگر 'victorious/triumphant' (adjective/noun used as epithet).
segments.s394-g16: همی یابی is an archaic present/future 2nd-person form 'you will/shall find/obtain' where همی is an archaic progressive/emphatic prefix. از اختر نیک بر is a short prepositional phrase 'from (an) auspicious/good star (upon you)' — اختر 'star,' نیک 'good/auspicious,' and بر here functions as a prepositional particle 'upon/over' used in older poetic syntax.
segments.s394-g81: This clause begins with the preposition به used here as a causal/agentive locution ('by/through'). داد immediately after به is the noun 'justice' (به دادِ آفریننده = 'by the Creator's justice'). The following داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (classical Persian often repeats forms), and راد is an epithet/noun 'the generous one' (here functioning as an appositive or subject: 'the generous [one]'). Word order is compressed and poetic: prepositional phrase indicating cause → noun (justice) → verb (gave) → epithet/subject.
segments.s394-g82: مرا here is the object 'me' (written in two parts in the word list: م + را). پاک جام 'pure cup' places پاک (pure) before جام (cup) in a compact noun phrase; و links the two direct objects. پرستنده is 'worshipper/devotee' (a person given as a companion or servant). داد at the end is the main past verb 'gave' governing مرا as its object. The overall structure: (Subject implied from previous segment) gave me (مرا) a pure cup and a worshipper.
segments.s394-g83: یکی here means 'a certain' (introducing one item). جام is 'cup' modified by the following postpositive or attributive phrases: پر باده = 'full of wine' (پر = 'full (of)') and مشک بوی = 'musk-scented' (literally 'musk-smelling'). Persian often strings modifiers after the head noun in poetry, so the sequence reads 'a certain cup, full of wine, musk-scented.'
segments.s394-g84: بدو داد = 'he gave it to him' where بدو is the clitic/compound meaning 'to him/unto it' and داد is the past verb 'gave'. تا introduces a purposive/result clause 'so that/in order that'. لعل = 'ruby' and گرددش is an older/poetic form meaning 'becomes it' (گردد + enclitic -ش = 'it becomes' / 'his becomes'); روی = 'face'. The clause therefore reads 'so that his face may become a ruby.'
segments.s394-g85: یکی = 'a certain (one)'. نغز here is an evaluative adjective 'delightful/finely made' modifying پولاد زنجیر = 'steel chain' (پولاد = 'steel', زنجیر = 'chain'). داشت is the past-tense verb 'had'. Word order: indefinite subject → descriptive noun phrase → past verb.
segments.s394-g86: نهان کرده is a past participial construction 'hidden' / 'having hidden' (نهان = 'hidden', کرده as the participial/auxiliary element). از + جادو = 'from magic' (i.e., concealed against magical forces), آژیر is 'siren' or 'alarm' (a device or signal), and داشت closes the clause with the past 'had'. The sequence means 'he had an alarm hidden from magic.'
segments.s394-g87: به بازوش = 'on his arm' (به marking location + بازو 'arm' + enclitic -ش 'his'). در بسته literally 'closed/ sealed door' — in this context poetically describing a sealed clasp or ring ('درِ بسته' = sealed opening). بد here is a poetic spelling representing بود (was) in past narrative context (common in classical verse). زردهشت = 'Zoroaster' (the prophet). So the clause reads 'on his arm there was a sealed clasp of Zoroaster' — note the archaic/poetic breve بد for بود.
segments.s394-g88: به گشتاسپ = 'to/for Goshtasp' (به as dative), آورده is the past participle/verb 'brought', and بود here is the auxiliary forming the past perfect ('had brought' — آورده بود). از بهشت = 'from paradise.' Word order is poetic: dative + recipient → participle → auxiliary → source.

sentences.s394-s57: May a proud hero remain in the world; may the world be in need of your love.
sentences.s394-s58: A certain flame sprang up from the (wolf‑browed) temple of the ferocious one; it arose from Esfandiyar's battle.
sentences.s394-s17: When the day put on a yellow robe, the bright world turned toward the west.
sentences.s394-s18: He raised the host and set up a bundle (camp) upon it; he remembered the gift of goodness from God.
sentences.s394-s19: In the dark night the king kept driving the host; when the sun appeared he donned a golden helmet.
sentences.s394-s20: When the tower's face became like a ruby, the whole face of the earth laughed.
sentences.s394-s53: A gust and a black cloud of dust arose, and it covered the sight of the sun and the moon.
sentences.s394-s54: The world‑seeking man rose up on high; like roaring thunder he uttered a single cry.
sentences.s394-s55: Peshotan came with his host, and thus spoke: 'O renowned king...'
sentences.s394-s56: Neither whale nor Turk, neither sorcery, nor lion nor leopard can stand against your wound.
sentences.s394-s21: They entrusted the whole army to Peshotan; one of them carried off a golden cup filled with wine.
sentences.s394-s22: Another requested a finely made tanbur as well; as the battle drew near to him, he asked for a feast.
sentences.s394-s23: One saw a grove like paradise; one would say the heavens had planted tulips in it.
sentences.s394-s24: He could not see the sun through the trees inside it; at every spot there was a spring like rosewater.
sentences.s394-s25: He alighted from the foliage; as was fitting, he chose the edge of a spring in the grove.
sentences.s394-s26: He set a certain golden cup upon his palm, when he realized that, from wine, his heart had grown glad.
sentences.s394-s27: At once he took up the tanbur; he began to sing and to utter laments.
sentences.s394-s28: Thus spoke ill‑fated Esfandiyar: 'that he will never see wine and a wine‑companion.'
sentences.s394-s1: Because of that deed the wolf-haunted place became full of grief; where had Esfandiyār — who was alive — become dead?
sentences.s394-s2: They struck the inner curtain at the king’s water‑bank; all the tents moved about within the host.
sentences.s394-s3: He set wine and fruit on the table and summoned the tippler; remembering the sovereign, he rose to his feet.
sentences.s394-s4: He commanded that the grievous wound of the wolf‑haunted heart be brought; a youth came into Esfandiyār’s presence.
sentences.s394-s9: When you come to the dwelling tomorrow, descend; a sorceress will come before you and bring a greeting.
sentences.s394-s10: It appears that before now many a host has been seen; no one has turned (their) course aside.
sentences.s394-s11: When it wills, it makes the wilderness like the sea; when it wills, it stretches its expanse up to the height of the sun.
sentences.s394-s12: They call him a ghoul; the kings call him by name. O Beh-ruz, young man, do not go before the snare.
sentences.s394-s13: You must not return in triumph to the dragon; do not put its name about.
sentences.s394-s14: The world-seeker said, 'O ill, impudent-faced one, whatever you see of me, tell it tomorrow.'
sentences.s394-s15: That I, with the enchanted woman, will do that — so that I may break the enchanters' back and heart.
sentences.s394-s16: A god who has granted victory — I will lay the enchanters' head at (my) feet.
sentences.s394-s45: That iron took from Esfandiyār's life a foreboding of ill fate.
sentences.s394-s46: He threw a chain about his neck in such a way that it would take the strength from his body.
sentences.s394-s47: The sorceress turned herself into a lion; the world‑seeker made as if to strike with his sword.
sentences.s394-s48: He said to him, 'You will not bring harm to me, even if you are an iron, a lofty mountain.'
sentences.s394-s49: Dress yourself so that you are clothed; now I will answer you with the sword.
sentences.s394-s50: The great, aged man was put in chains and ruined; his head and hair were like snow and his complexion black.
sentences.s394-s51: Someone struck his head with a sharp dagger; beware lest you see his head cut off.
sentences.s394-s52: When the sorcerer died, the sky darkened, so that eyes were left staring at it.
sentences.s394-s29: She will see nothing except the lion and the male dragon; she will find no escape from the claws of calamity.
sentences.s394-s30: One will gain no share of this world by beholding the fortunate, fairy‑faced one.
sentences.s394-s31: May I obtain from God the desire of my heart, if He grants me that heart‑rending face.
sentences.s394-s32: On high her face was like a cypress and like the sun; musk was scattered down to the ends of her hair.
sentences.s394-s37: He came up close to Esfandiyar and sat down beside the green and the stream.
sentences.s394-s38: When the world‑seeker saw his face, he burst into song and lifted wine and the lute higher.
sentences.s394-s39: Thus he said: 'O just one, one God — be my guide in the mountains and the wilderness.'
sentences.s394-s40: Just now I have found a fairy‑faced one; famed in body — from her I have a portion.
sentences.s394-s33: When the sorceress heard Esfandiyar's voice, she became like a flower in spring.
sentences.s394-s34: Thus it was said that Hezbar came into the trap; Aba, Chameh and Rud filled the cup.
sentences.s394-s35: On that ugly, uncouth face, full of azhang, he inscribed the darkness of the sorceries.
sentences.s394-s36: Like a Turk, a fair-faced man appeared; his face was like Chinese brocade and his hair scented with musk.
sentences.s394-s5: In kingly fashion he gave his three cups; he laughed, and from that he remembered the dragon.
sentences.s394-s6: He said to him, 'O base-bodied, worthless one! Look now — behold the male dragon's cry.'
sentences.s394-s7: From now on, to what dwelling shall I come, and where will greater suffering and care befall me?
sentences.s394-s8: He said to him, 'O victorious king, you shall surely obtain (favor) from a good/auspicious star.'
sentences.s394-s41: By the Creator's justice and generosity he gave me a pure cup and a worshipper.
sentences.s394-s42: He gave a certain cup, full of wine and scented with musk, to him, so that his face might become like a ruby.
sentences.s394-s43: A certain man had a fine steel chain; he had an alarm (siren) hidden from magic.
sentences.s394-s44: On his arm was a sealed clasp (or ring) that Zoroaster had brought to Goshtasp from paradise.
